I have minutely perused the record and considered their rival submissions. My issue-wise finding are recorded below: 23. Issue No.1 - As per terms of reference? OPW 24. It is settled law that the provisions of per se are not applicable in the industrial adjudications. Page:9 of 22 Bhagirathi Mal Vs. M/s S. Chand and Company Ltd. & Anr. LIR No.1399/2019 The general principles of it are, however, applicable and the principles of natural justice are complied with. 25. It is well settled principle of law that a person who sets up a plea of existence of employer-employee relation is required to adduce cogent evidence for discharging the burden as held by Hon'ble Supreme Court of India in 'Workmen of Nilgiri Co- operative Marketing

case_name: Bhagirathi Mal vs M/S S.Chand And Company Ltd on 29 March, 2025
text: justice are complied with. 25. It is well settled principle of law that a person who sets up a plea of existence of employer-employee relation is required to adduce cogent evidence for discharging the burden as held by Hon'ble Supreme Court of India in 'Workmen of Nilgiri Co- operative Marketing Society Vs. State of Tamil Nadu and Ors. (2004) 3 SCC 514'. Paras 47 to 49 of the judgment of Hon'ble Apex Court being relevant are extracted below: 47. It is a well-settled principle of law that the person who sets up a plea of existence of relationship of employer and employee, the burden would be upon him. 48. In N.C. John Vs. Secy, Thodupuzha Taluk Shop and Commercial Establishment Workers' Union, the Kerala High Court held: The burden of proof being on the workmen to establish the employer-employee relationship an adverse inference cannot be drawn against the employer that if he were to produce books of accounts they would have proved employer-employee relationship. 49. In Swapan Das Gupta Vs. The First Labour Court of West Bengal it has been held: Where a person asserts that he was a workmen of the Company, and it is denied by the Company,

case_name: Bhagirathi Mal vs M/S S.Chand And Company Ltd on 29 March, 2025
text: if he were to produce books of accounts they would have proved employer-employee relationship. 49. In Swapan Das Gupta Vs. The First Labour Court of West Bengal it has been held: Where a person asserts that he was a workmen of the Company, and it is denied by the Company, it is for him to prove the fact. It is not for the Company to prove that he was not an employee of the Company but of some other person. Page:10 of 22 Bhagirathi Mal Vs. M/s S. Chand and Company Ltd. & Anr. LIR No.1399/2019 26. Scope and ambit of , Section 25B and of I.D Act was considered by Hon'ble Supreme Court of India in para 6 of judgment in "Surendernagar District Panchayat Vs. Dahyabhai Amarsinh" (2005) 8 SCC 750 for observing that workman claiming protection under of I.D. Act has to prove (i) existence of employer-employee relation; (ii) employment as workman within the meaning of of I.D. Act; (iii) establishment being an industry under of I.D. Act AND (iv) continuous service under the employer as defined under of I.D. Act. Existence of Employer-Employee Relationship 27. The contention of management no.1 is that no employer-employee relationship exist between

Act. Existence of Employer-Employee Relationship 27. The contention of management no.1 is that no employer-employee relationship exist between workman and management no.1, hence, workman is not entitled to seek relief against it. In support of his contention, the AR for management no.1 has relied upon the judgment of Hon'ble High Court of Delhi titled as "Airport Authority of India Vs. A.S. Yadav and Ors., W.P. (C) 5168/2005 and CM No.47971/2029" wherein the Hon'ble High Court of Delhi has made reference to paragraphs 37 to 39 of the decision in "International Airport Authority of India V. International Cargo Workers' Union and Another, (2009) 13SCC 374" which reads as under :- "37. The industrial adjudicator can grant the relief sought if it finds that contract between the principal employer and the contractor is sham, nominal and merely a camouflage to deny employment benefits to the employer and that there is in fact a direct employment, by applying Page:11 of 22 Bhagirathi Mal Vs. M/s S. Chand and Company Ltd. &

text: if it finds that contract between the principal employer and the contractor is sham, nominal and merely a camouflage to deny employment benefits to the employer and that there is in fact a direct employment, by applying Page:11 of 22 Bhagirathi Mal Vs. M/s S. Chand and Company Ltd. & Anr. LIR No.1399/2019 tests like: who pays the salary; who has the power to remove/dismiss from service or initiate disciplinary action; who has direction and employee the way in which the work should be done, in short, who has direction and control over the employee. But where there is no notification under of the CLRA Act and where it is not proved in the industrial adjudication that the contract was a sham/nominal and camouflage, then the question of directing the principal employer to absorb or regularize the services of the contract labour does not arise. 38. The tests that are applies to find out whether a person is an employee or an independent contractor may not automatically apply in finding out whether the contract labour agreement is a sham, nominal and is a mere camouflage. For example, if the contract is for supply of labour, necessarily, the labour supplied by

case_name: Bhagirathi Mal vs M/S S.Chand And Company Ltd on 29 March, 2025
text: applies to find out whether a person is an employee or an independent contractor may not automatically apply in finding out whether the contract labour agreement is a sham, nominal and is a mere camouflage. For example, if the contract is for supply of labour, necessarily, the labour supplied by the contractor will work under the directions, supervision and control of the principal employer, if the salary is paid by a contractor, if the right to regulate the employment is with the contractor and the ultimate supervision and control lies with the contractor. 39. The principal employer only controls and directs the work to be done by a contract labour, when such labour is assigned/allotted/allotted/sent to him. But it is the contractor as employer, who choose whether the worker is to be assigned/allotted to the principal employer or used otherwise. In short, Page:12 of 22 Bhagirathi Mal Vs. M/s S. Chand and Company Ltd. & Anr. LIR No.1399/2019 worker being the employee of the contractor, the ultimate supervision and control lies with the contractor as he decides where the employee will work and how long he will work and subject to what conditions. Only when the contractor assigns/sends the worker

case_name: Bhagirathi Mal vs M/S S.Chand And Company Ltd on 29 March, 2025
text: Chand and Company Ltd. & Anr. LIR No.1399/2019 worker being the employee of the contractor, the ultimate supervision and control lies with the contractor as he decides where the employee will work and how long he will work and subject to what conditions. Only when the contractor assigns/sends the worker to work under the principal employer, the worker works under the supervision and control of the principal employer but that is secondary control. The primary control is with the contractor." 28. Relying upon Airport Authority case (Supra) management no.1 contended that in a dispute of existence of employer-employee relationship, the court adjudicating the dispute is required to examine as to (1) who pays salary to the workman, (2) who possesses the power to initiate disciplinary action against him and (3) whether the alleged employer has any control and supervision over the claimant workman. 29. In 'Balwant Rai Saluja and Another Vs. Air India Limited and Others (2014 (9) SCC 407)' the Hon'ble Supreme Court held as under :- "65. Thus, it can be concluded that the relevant factors to be taken into consideration to establish an employer- employee relationship would include, inter alia:" "(i) who appoints the workers; (ii) who

case_name: Bhagirathi Mal vs M/S S.Chand And Company Ltd on 29 March, 2025
text: Vs. Air India Limited and Others (2014 (9) SCC 407)' the Hon'ble Supreme Court held as under :- "65. Thus, it can be concluded that the relevant factors to be taken into consideration to establish an employer- employee relationship would include, inter alia:" "(i) who appoints the workers; (ii) who pays the salary/remuneration; (iii) who has the authority to dismiss; (iv) who can take disciplinary action; (v) whether there is continuity of service; and (vi) extent of control and supervision i.e. whether there exists complete control Page:13 of 22 Bhagirathi Mal Vs. M/s S. Chand and Company Ltd. & Anr. LIR No.1399/2019 and supervision." 30. In the present case, the evidence on record shows that the workman was employed by management no.2 and deputed to work at management no.1. The terms of employment including salary are managed by management no.2. All employment related documents such as appointment letter, salary slip and I-Card were issued by management no.2. Management no.1 did not issue any appointment letter, salary slip, I-Card or other employment related documents to the workman. 31. The instructions issued by the employees of management no.1 at the workplace/management no.1 does not amount to control and supervision as held by

text: and I-Card were issued by management no.2. Management no.1 did not issue any appointment letter, salary slip, I-Card or other employment related documents to the workman. 31. The instructions issued by the employees of management no.1 at the workplace/management no.1 does not amount to control and supervision as held by Hon'ble High Court of Delhi in Airports Authority of India case (Supra). The ultimate control and supervision was with the management no.2. 32. There is no direct contractual relationship between the workman and management no.1, therefor, no employer-employee relationship exist between workman and management no.1. 33. Since there is no employer-employee relationship exist between workman and management no.1, the claim petition of workman is not maintainable against management no.1. Claim barred by of ESI Act, 1948? 34. of The Employees' State Insurance Act, 1948 reads as under:- 53. Bar against receiving or recovery of compensation or damages under any other law. "An insured person or his dependents shall not be entitled to receive or recover, whether from the employer of Page:14 of 22 Bhagirathi Mal Vs. M/s S. Chand and Company Ltd. & Anr. LIR No.1399/2019 the insured person or from any other person, any compensation or damages under the

text: law. "An insured person or his dependents shall not be entitled to receive or recover, whether from the employer of Page:14 of 22 Bhagirathi Mal Vs. M/s S. Chand and Company Ltd. & Anr. LIR No.1399/2019 the insured person or from any other person, any compensation or damages under the or any other law for the time being in force or otherwise, in respect of an employment injury sustained by the insured person as an employee under this Act." 35. On careful perusal of the said provisions I find that the provides for disability benefits under the Act but do not preclude the workman seeking relief under for illegal termination. The claim pertains to retrenchment and reinstatement under the and not to benefits under the . 36. Hence, the contention of management no.2 is hereby rejected. Whether workman absconded from duty? 37. It is settled position of law that abandonment of service is the voluntarily relinquishment of ones service with the intention not to resume the same and it is different from absenteeism. Abandonment has to be seen from facts and circumstances of each case. 38. In M/s Fateh Chand Vs. Presiding Offcer, Labour Court & Anr, W.P. (C) No.758/2007

case_name: Bhagirathi Mal vs M/S S.Chand And Company Ltd on 29 March, 2025
text: of service is the voluntarily relinquishment of ones service with the intention not to resume the same and it is different from absenteeism. Abandonment has to be seen from facts and circumstances of each case. 38. In M/s Fateh Chand Vs. Presiding Offcer, Labour Court & Anr, W.P. (C) No.758/2007 the Hon'ble High Court of Delhi held that : "8.....It is also a settled legal position that abandonment of service is different from absenteeism. Abandonment of service is the voluntarily relinquishment of ones services with the intention not to resume the same. It is a matter of inference to be drawn from the facts and Page:15 of 22 Bhagirathi Mal Vs. M/s S. Chand and Company Ltd. & Anr. LIR No.1399/2019 circumstances of each case and mere absenteeism for a continuous period does not mean that the employee has abandoned his service. The management has to bring on record sufficient material to show that the employee has abandoned the service and abandonment cannot be attributed to the employee without there being sufficient evidence. On the failure to report for duty, the management has to call upon the employee and if he refuses to report, then an enquiry is required to

case_name: Bhagirathi Mal vs M/S S.Chand And Company Ltd on 29 March, 2025
text: material to show that the employee has abandoned the service and abandonment cannot be attributed to the employee without there being sufficient evidence. On the failure to report for duty, the management has to call upon the employee and if he refuses to report, then an enquiry is required to be ordered against him and accordingly action taken. In the absence of anything placed on record by the petitioner management, no presumption against the respondent can be drawn." 39. It is undisputed fact that the workman suffered a serious back injury during the course of his employment which rendered him permanently partial disabled for physical labour. His absence from work was due to this injury. Both the managements were aware of workman's injury. Hence, workman has sufficient reason for non-joining the duties. 40. Accordingly, there is no evidence on record to show that the workman has absconded from his duty. Whether services of workman terminated illegally? 41. of the Industrial Disputes Act defines retrenchment as under: "2[(oo) "retrenchment" means the termination by the employer of the service of a workman for any reason whatsoever, otherwise than as a punishment inflicted by way of disciplinary Page:16 of 22 Bhagirathi Mal Vs.

case_name: Bhagirathi Mal vs M/S S.Chand And Company Ltd on 29 March, 2025
text: Whether services of workman terminated illegally? 41. of the Industrial Disputes Act defines retrenchment as under: "2[(oo) "retrenchment" means the termination by the employer of the service of a workman for any reason whatsoever, otherwise than as a punishment inflicted by way of disciplinary Page:16 of 22 Bhagirathi Mal Vs. M/s S. Chand and Company Ltd. & Anr. LIR No.1399/2019 action, but does not include - (a) voluntary retirement of the workman; or (b) retirement of the workman on reaching the age of superannuation if the contract of employment between the employer and the workman concerned contains a stipulation in that behalf; or [(bb) termination of the service of the workman as a result of the non- renewal of the contract of employment between the employer and the workman concerned on its expiry or of such contract being terminated under a stipulation in that behalf contained therein; or] (c) termination of the service of a workman on the ground of continued ill- health;] 42. defines retrenchment as the termination by the employer of the service of a workman for any reason whatsoever, otherwise than as a punishment inflicted by way of disciplinary action. However, it excludes certain categories of termination

case_name: Bhagirathi Mal vs M/S S.Chand And Company Ltd on 29 March, 2025
text: of the service of a workman on the ground of continued ill- health;] 42. defines retrenchment as the termination by the employer of the service of a workman for any reason whatsoever, otherwise than as a punishment inflicted by way of disciplinary action. However, it excludes certain categories of termination from the definition of retrenchment, including: i.e. "termination of the service of the workman on the ground of continued ill-health." 43. of Industrial Disputes Act, 1947 provides conditions precedent to retrenchment to workman as under: 25F. Conditions precedent to retrenchment of workmen. "No workman employed in any industry who has been in continuous service for not less than one year under an employer Page:17 of 22 Bhagirathi Mal Vs. M/s S. Chand and Company Ltd. & Anr. LIR No.1399/2019 shall be retrenched by that employer until: (a) The workman has been given one month 's notice in writing indicating the reasons for retrenchment and the period of notice has expired, or the workman has been paid in lieu of such notice, wages for the period of the notice. (b) The workman has been paid, at the time of retrenchment, compensation which shall be equivalent to fifteen days 'average pay or

case_name: Bhagirathi Mal vs M/S S.Chand And Company Ltd on 29 March, 2025
text: reasons for retrenchment and the period of notice has expired, or the workman has been paid in lieu of such notice, wages for the period of the notice. (b) The workman has been paid, at the time of retrenchment, compensation which shall be equivalent to fifteen days 'average pay or any part thereof in excess of six months; and (c) Notice in the prescribed manner is served on the appropriate Government [or such authority as may be specified by the appropriate Government by notification in the Official Gazette." 44. of the Industrial Disputes Act mandates that an employer must provide one months notice or wages in lieu of notice and compensation equivalent to 15 days' average pay for every completed year of service before retrenching an employee. 45. In " , AIR 1991 SC 1003" the Hon'ble Supreme Court held as under: "The expression ill-health used in sub- clause (c) of of the Industrial Disputes Act, 1947 has to be construed relatively and in its context. It must have a bearing on the normal discharge of duties. It is not any illness but that which interferes with the usual orderly functioning of the duties of the Page:18 of 22 Bhagirathi

case_name: Bhagirathi Mal vs M/S S.Chand And Company Ltd on 29 March, 2025
text: of of the Industrial Disputes Act, 1947 has to be construed relatively and in its context. It must have a bearing on the normal discharge of duties. It is not any illness but that which interferes with the usual orderly functioning of the duties of the Page:18 of 22 Bhagirathi Mal Vs. M/s S. Chand and Company Ltd. & Anr. LIR No.1399/2019 post which would be attracted by the sub-clause. Conversely, even if the illness does not affect general health or general capacity and is restricted only to a particular limb or organ but affects the efficient working of the work entrusted it will be covered by the phrase. For it is not the capacity in general but that which is necessary for perform the duty for which the workman is engaged which is relevant and material and should be considered for the purpose. Therefore, any disorder in health which incapacitates an individual from discharging the duties entrusted to him or affects his work adversely or comes in the way of his normal and effective functioning can be covered by the said phrase." 46. In view of above settled position of law, the ill-health must be such that it affect

case_name: Bhagirathi Mal vs M/S S.Chand And Company Ltd on 29 March, 2025
text: incapacitates an individual from discharging the duties entrusted to him or affects his work adversely or comes in the way of his normal and effective functioning can be covered by the said phrase." 46. In view of above settled position of law, the ill-health must be such that it affect the efficient working of the duties entrusted to the workman and it is not the general capacity of the workman but the capacity to perform the specific duties for which the workman is engaged. Further, if the illness or disability prevents the workman from performing the duties for which he was employed, it may fall under the exception to retrenchment to of Industrial Disputes Act. 47. Accordingly, the workman's case may qualify as an exception to retrenchment under the . Thus, the termination of services by way of refusal of work by seeking physical fitness certificate from Government Hospital by management no.2, itself, is not illegal. Whether services of workman terminated unjustifiably? Page:19 of 22 Bhagirathi Mal Vs. M/s S. Chand and Company Ltd. & Anr. LIR No.1399/2019 48. In case (Supra) the Hon'ble court held that while the termination of employees per se was not illegal, it declared the

case_name: Bhagirathi Mal vs M/S S.Chand And Company Ltd on 29 March, 2025
text: management no.2, itself, is not illegal. Whether services of workman terminated unjustifiably? Page:19 of 22 Bhagirathi Mal Vs. M/s S. Chand and Company Ltd. & Anr. LIR No.1399/2019 48. In case (Supra) the Hon'ble court held that while the termination of employees per se was not illegal, it declared the termination in question unjustified and granted relief to the workman. The Hon'ble court emphasized that employers must act bonafide and cannot misuse statutory exceptions as a pretext to terminate a workman without first exploring alternative employment, if available. The Hon'ble court also observed: "5. No special provision is made and no compensatory relief is provided in the service condition for the drivers for premature incapacitation. The service conditions of the workmen such as the drivers in the present case, therefore, must provide for adequate safeguards to remedy the situation by compensating them is some form for the all-round loss they suffer for no fault of theirs." 49. Thus the Hon'ble court has not absolve the employer of its obligation to provide alternative employment if the workman remained capable of performing other duties. 50. In the present case, the workman suffered a serious back injury during the course of his employment

case_name: Bhagirathi Mal vs M/S S.Chand And Company Ltd on 29 March, 2025
text: suffer for no fault of theirs." 49. Thus the Hon'ble court has not absolve the employer of its obligation to provide alternative employment if the workman remained capable of performing other duties. 50. In the present case, the workman suffered a serious back injury during the course of his employment which has rendered him permanently partial disabled. His absence from work was due to this injury and the resulting ill-health. The workman cannot stand straight or perform the duties of a helper which involve physical work. However, the workman has stated that he is capable of performing sedentary work, like telephone operator, etc. 51. In the reply filed by the management no.2, it offered to reinstate the workman at another location subject to production of Page:20 of 22 Bhagirathi Mal Vs. M/s S. Chand and Company Ltd. & Anr. LIR No.1399/2019 physical fitness certificate. The above said facts clearly indicates that the management no.2 has acknowledged the workman's right to employment subject to the production of physical fitness certificate. The management no.2 has sought physical fitness certificate as per the terms of employment between the workman and management no.2. 52. It is admitted case of the workman/WW-1 that he has

case_name: Bhagirathi Mal vs M/S S.Chand And Company Ltd on 29 March, 2025
text: that the management no.2 has acknowledged the workman's right to employment subject to the production of physical fitness certificate. The management no.2 has sought physical fitness certificate as per the terms of employment between the workman and management no.2. 52. It is admitted case of the workman/WW-1 that he has not produced any fitness certificate for rejoining duty. 53. The workman's inability to produce the physical fitness certificate is due to his permanent partial disability which was caused by a work related injury. While the workman's disability prevents him from performing the duty of a helper, he is capable of performing alternative work. Therefore, non- production of physical fitness certificate does not absolve the management no.2 to provide alternative employment that accommodates the workman's disability. 54. In the employment agreement between the workman and management no.2 no special provision or compensatory relief is provided for premature incapacitation. The management no.2 has not provided adequate safeguard in the terms of employment to remedy the present situation of the workman in some form for the loss he suffer for no fault at his own. 55. The workman is at the advance stage of his life having wife, school going children and old

case_name: Bhagirathi Mal vs M/S S.Chand And Company Ltd on 29 March, 2025
text: has not provided adequate safeguard in the terms of employment to remedy the present situation of the workman in some form for the loss he suffer for no fault at his own. 55. The workman is at the advance stage of his life having wife, school going children and old age parents and it would be difficult for him to get a suitable alternative employment outside Delhi. The workman is unfit merely for physical labour, however, he retains the capacity to undertake sedentary employment Page:21 of 22 Bhagirathi Mal Vs. M/s S. Chand and Company Ltd. & Anr. LIR No.1399/2019 including but not limited to work as telephone operator, etc. 56. Accordingly, keeping in view the peculiar facts of the case, I am of the considered opinion that the refusal of work by management no.2 by way of asking physical fitness certificate from the workman amounts to unjustified termination of his services. RELIEF: 57. Management no.2 is directed to provide suitable alternate employment to workman, such as telephone operator, etc. or any sedentary work within Delhi, if available, within 30 days of passing of this Award. If not available, then management no.2 shall pay a lump sum amount of Rs.4,50,000/-
